raw_case:
A Novel Steroidogenic Acute Regulatory Protein (StAR) Mutation Causing Adrenal Insufficiency in a Neonate: A Case Report of a Rare Medical Condition
Congenital lipoid adrenal hyperplasia is a very rare and severe cause of adrenal insufficiency. It occurs due to a mutation of the steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR), disrupting adrenal steroid biosynthesis. Here, we report a case of a three-week-old female infant with vomiting, failure to thrive, electrolyte imbalance, and generalized hyperpigmentation. The hormonal assay and genetic diagnosis confirmed a mutation in the StAR protein, leading to adrenal insufficiency. Appropriate replacement therapy resulted in the resolution of clinical and biochemical abnormalities. This case is being reported for its rare etiology and diagnostic clues. It can guide clinicians to keep adrenal insufficiency as a differential diagnosis in a neonate presenting with hyperpigmentation and electrolyte disturbance to save lives.

Introduction

Congenital lipoid adrenal hyperplasia (CLAH) is an extremely rare subtype of congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH). The first step in the adrenal and gonadal steroid synthetic pathway is blocked, leading to the nonconversion of cholesterol to pregnenolone [1]. The incidence of this genetic disorder is 1 in 250,000 to 1 in 300,000 newborns, and it is more frequent in the Japanese and Korean populations [2]. There is a progressive accumulation of cholesterol in the adrenal gland and the deficient synthesis of glucocorticoids, mineralocorticoids, and androgens culminating in hypoglycemia, electrolyte imbalance, abnormal male external genitalia, and adrenal hypertrophy in the neonate [3]. Here, we present a case of a three-week-old neonate who presented with symptoms of vomiting, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia due to this rare genetic disorder.

Case presentation

A female infant with a birth weight of 3 kg was born to a primigravida mother at 37 weeks of gestational age, with no antenatal or postnatal complications. On day 22, the baby was admitted to our hospital with lethargy and vomiting. On examination, the baby was hyporesponsive with a heart rate of 145/minute, respiratory rate of 55/minute, and well palpable pulses. Random blood sugar was 28 mg/dL, for which a bolus of 10% dextrose at 2 mL/kg was given. IV fluids with a glucose infusion rate (GIR) of 6 mg/kg/minute were started. The skin had generalized dark pigmentation, which parents complained was progressively increasing, as skin color was fair at birth, as shown in Figure 1.Normal external female genitalia was present with no palpable abdominal or inguinal mass, as shown in Figure 2.There was no family history of similar conditions, any endocrine diseases, or neonatal deaths. The infant had failed to thrive as well, with 23% weight loss since birth. Initial investigation ruled out sepsis and meningitis, but hyponatremia and hyperkalemia with acute kidney injury were noted, as shown in Table 1.Slow dehydration correction was administered over 72 hours, and electrolytes were monitored 12 hours a day. Renal functions showed gradual improvement, but hyponatremia and hyperkalemia persisted even after sodium supplementation and administration of potassium-free fluids. There were episodes of intermittent asymptomatic hypoglycemia, mandating an increase of GIR up to 10 mg/kg/minute.Considering the constellation of features like dark pigmentation of the skin, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia, and failure to thrive, a diagnosis of adrenal insufficiency was made. Additional hormonal levels are summarized in Table 2.Ultrasound of the abdomen showed bilaterally enlarged adrenal glands and the presence of Müllerian structures. On day 25, the infant was started on a standard regimen of hydrocortisone at 20 mg/m2/day, fludrocortisone at 0.1 mg/day, and sodium supplementation at 3 mEq/kg/day. There was a gradual improvement toward normalization of sodium and potassium, as shown in Table 1. The baby became euglycemic and was able to consume full feeds.Given multiple episodes of hypoglycemia, an MRI of the brain was done, which showed T2 fluid-attenuated inversion recovery hyperintensities in the occipital region with diffusion restriction, mostly due to hypoglycemic insult to the brain. A genetic study with targeted gene sequencing was done, which gave a final diagnosis of lipoid CAH (LCAH) due to a homozygous mutation of the steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR) gene with autosomal recessive inheritance.The baby was discharged by day 10 on continued oral hydrocortisone, fludrocortisone, and sodium supplements. The parents were explained genetic counseling before the next pregnancy. On follow-up, after two weeks of discharge, the baby was euglycemic with an adequate weight gain of 20 mg/kg/day and normal electrolytes, as depicted in Table 1.

Discussion

CLAH is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by a mutation in the StAR gene. The function of StAR is the transport of cholesterol from the outer membrane to the inner membrane of mitochondria so that it can be cleaved into pregnenolone [4]. As a result, further hormone biosynthesis does not occur in the adrenals, leading to a myriad of clinical manifestations. The symptomatology, age of onset, and severity of LCAH varied significantly in cases reported in the literature except for genital phenotype, which is similar in both sexes [5].The deficiency of glucocorticoids manifests as hypoglycemia with low cortisol levels, as was seen in our case. The mineralocorticoid deficiency is depicted by low aldosterone levels with hyponatremia and hyperkalemia, which was refractory to general electrolyte correction measures in our case, like sodium supplementation and potassium-free fluid. Massive adrenal enlargement is classically seen in LCAH, but some cases have also reported normal or small adrenal gland size [6]. The external genital was of a normal female, and the ultrasound confirmed the presence of Müllerian structures, indicating the female gender. Gender assignment is crucial in CLAH, as neonates with XY karyotypes can have female external genitalia due to the absence of testosterone [7].Generalized hyperpigmentation served as an important clue for diagnosis. Due to the absence of negative feedback by adrenal hormones, adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) secretion remains unchecked from the anterior pituitary, which causes hyperpigmentation by complementing the increased activity of melanocyte-stimulating hormone [8].In the most common form of CAH, 21-hydroxylase deficiency, 17-hydroxyprogesterone levels are high, but in our case, it was low, which was a differentiating point. Low levels of 17-hydroxyprogesterone are seen in lipoid adrenal hyperplasia, 17,20 lyase deficiency, and 17-alpha-hydroxylase enzyme deficiency [9].Treatment for LCAH aims to correct the deficiency in cortisol and aldosterone secretion and suppress ACTH overproduction [10]. In our case, the baby responded very well clinically and biochemically to hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone. Lifelong replacement therapy is needed in such cases. LCAH, being an autosomal recessive disorder, has a 25% chance of recurrence; hence, appropriate antenatal counseling should be done in future pregnancies.

Conclusions

LCAH is an extremely rare form of CAH that can be missed in newborn screening due to low 17-hydroxyprogesterone levels. Generalized hyperpigmentation with hyponatremia and hyperkalemia should alert the clinician to look for adrenal insufficiency rather than providing routine symptomatic therapy. If undiagnosed, this disease if untreated can turn fatal as the child may go into an adrenal crisis. Thus, clinicians must be very vigilant as early diagnosis and hormonal replacement therapy can be lifesaving for such babies.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** A three-week-old female neonate.
- **Chief Complaint:** Vomiting, lethargy.
- **History of Present Illness:** Progressive skin darkening and failure to thrive noted since birth.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** None.
- **Family History:** No history of similar conditions, endocrine diseases, or neonatal deaths.
- **Physical Examination:** Generalized dark skin pigmentation, normal female external genitalia, and no palpable abdominal or inguinal masses.
- **Ancillary Tests:**  
  - Blood tests: Hypoglycemia (random blood sugar 28 mg/dL), persistent hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and intermittent hypoglycemia despite therapy.  
  - Ultrasound: Bilaterally enlarged adrenal glands and presence of Müllerian structures.  
  - MRI of the brain: Occipital T2 hyperintensities indicating hypoglycemic insult.  
  - Hormonal studies: Low 17-hydroxyprogesterone levels, cortisol deficiency, and aldosterone deficiency.  
  - Genetic testing: Homozygous mutation in the steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR) gene with autosomal recessive inheritance.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial considerations:**  
   - The patient's symptoms (vomiting, hyperpigmentation, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia, and failure to thrive) suggest adrenal insufficiency.  
   - Differential diagnoses included common forms of congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) such as 21-hydroxylase deficiency, 17,20 lyase deficiency, or 17-alpha-hydroxylase deficiency, as well as other causes of neonatal adrenal insufficiency like congenital lipoid adrenal hyperplasia (CLAH).

2. **Inclusion of CLAH:**  
   - Initial clinical clues emphasized generalized skin hyperpigmentation due to unchecked ACTH activity.  
   - Persistently low levels of 17-hydroxyprogesterone differentiated CLAH from the more common 21-hydroxylase deficiency.  
   - Enlarged adrenal glands on imaging were consistent with cholesterol accumulation seen in CLAH.  
   - Genetic testing confirmed a homozygous StAR mutation, solidifying the diagnosis.

3. **Exclusion of other causes:**  
   - Classic 21-hydroxylase deficiency was ruled out based on low 17-hydroxyprogesterone levels.  
   - 17,20 lyase and 17-alpha-hydroxylase deficiencies were excluded due to the absence of other enzyme-specific deficiencies.  
   - Sepsis and meningitis were ruled out early via initial investigations.  

4. **Final Confirmation:** Hormonal studies, imaging, and genetic testing collectively confirmed congenital lipoid adrenal hyperplasia due to a StAR mutation as the definitive diagnosis.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
Congenital Lipoid Adrenal Hyperplasia (CLAH) due to a homozygous mutation in the steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR) gene.

---

checked_rare_disease: Congenital lipoid adrenal hyperplasia due to STAR deficency

results.content:
### Reasoning:
<step 1> **Initial Symptom Assessment:** The neonate presents with vomiting, lethargy, progressive skin darkening, and failure to thrive since birth. These are concerning symptoms in a newborn, suggesting a systemic issue, possibly metabolic or endocrine. Vomiting and lethargy in a neonate are non-specific but warrant immediate investigation. Skin darkening (hyperpigmentation) is a key finding that directs attention towards potential adrenal dysfunction. Failure to thrive indicates a lack of adequate growth and development, further emphasizing a serious underlying problem.

<step 2> **Physical Examination Findings Analysis:** Generalized dark skin pigmentation strongly suggests increased melanocyte-stimulating hormone (MSH) production, which is often secondary to elevated adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH). Elevated ACTH is a compensatory response to primary adrenal insufficiency, where the pituitary gland attempts to stimulate the adrenals to produce more cortisol. Normal female external genitalia is noted, which is important to consider in the context of potential congenital adrenal conditions. Absence of palpable abdominal or inguinal masses is helpful in ruling out certain structural abnormalities or tumors.

<step 3> **Blood Test Interpretation:** Hypoglycemia (very low blood sugar) is a critical finding in a neonate. Persistent hyponatremia and hyperkalemia are classic electrolyte disturbances indicative of mineralocorticoid (aldosterone) deficiency, a hallmark of adrenal insufficiency. Intermittent hypoglycemia despite therapy further suggests a fundamental hormonal or metabolic defect that is not easily corrected by simple glucose supplementation.

<step 4> **Ultrasound and MRI Findings Evaluation:** Bilaterally enlarged adrenal glands are highly suggestive of adrenal hyperplasia. In the context of suspected adrenal insufficiency, this is most likely due to chronic ACTH stimulation. The presence of Müllerian structures confirms the female sex internally, consistent with the normal female external genitalia. Occipital T2 hyperintensities on brain MRI indicate hypoglycemic brain injury, demonstrating the severity and impact of the hypoglycemia.

<step 5> **Hormonal Study Analysis:** Low 17-hydroxyprogesterone levels, cortisol deficiency, and aldosterone deficiency are a critical triad. Cortisol and aldosterone deficiencies directly confirm adrenal insufficiency. Low 17-hydroxyprogesterone levels, in the context of cortisol and aldosterone deficiency, suggest a defect early in the steroidogenesis pathway, before the production of 17-hydroxyprogesterone. This is important in differentiating between different types of congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH).

<step 6> **Genetic Testing Result Significance:** The homozygous mutation in the steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR) gene is the definitive piece of evidence. StAR protein is essential for the transport of cholesterol into the mitochondria, the rate-limiting step in the synthesis of all steroid hormones (including cortisol, aldosterone, and sex hormones). A homozygous mutation implies a complete or near-complete loss of StAR protein function, leading to a severe block in steroid hormone production. Autosomal recessive inheritance pattern is consistent with the family history (no prior similar conditions, suggesting parents are carriers).

<step 7> **Synthesizing Findings for Final Diagnosis:** Combining all the clinical, biochemical, radiological, and genetic findings, the most consistent diagnosis is **Lipoid Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (LCAH)**. LCAH is the most severe form of congenital adrenal hyperplasia, caused by mutations in the StAR gene. It results in a near-complete absence of all steroid hormones. The clinical presentation of vomiting, lethargy, skin darkening, failure to thrive, electrolyte imbalances (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia), hypoglycemia, adrenal hyperplasia, and the genetic confirmation of a homozygous StAR mutation are all classic features of LCAH. The normal female external genitalia and presence of Müllerian structures are also consistent with LCAH in a genotypic and phenotypic female.

### Answer:
Lipoid Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (LCAH)

**Genetic Testing Result Significance:** The homozygous mutation in the steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR) gene is the definitive piece of evidence. **Synthesizing Findings for Final Diagnosis:** Combining all the clinical, biochemical, radiological, and genetic findings, the most consistent diagnosis is **Lipoid Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (LCAH)**. LCAH is the most severe form of congenital adrenal hyperplasia, caused by mutations in the StAR gene. It results in a near-complete absence of all steroid hormones. The clinical presentation of vomiting, lethargy, skin darkening, failure to thrive, electrolyte imbalances (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia), hypoglycemia, adrenal hyperplasia, and the genetic confirmation of a homozygous StAR mutation are all classic features of LCAH. The normal female external genitalia and presence of Müllerian structures are also consistent with LCAH in a genotypic and phenotypic female.

<Step 2> Generalized skin hyperpigmentation, persistently low levels of 17-hydroxyprogesterone, and enlarged adrenal glands on imaging indicate CLAH over other congenital adrenal hyperplasias.
<Step 3> Genetic testing confirmed a homozygous StAR mutation, confirming the diagnosis of CLAH.
<Step 4> Classic 21-hydroxylase deficiency was ruled out by low 17-hydroxyprogesterone levels.
<Step 5> 17,20 lyase and 17-alpha-hydroxylase deficiencies were excluded due to the absence of other enzyme-specific deficiencies.
<Step 6> Sepsis and meningitis were ruled out early via initial investigations.
<Step 7> Hormonal studies, imaging, and genetic testing collectively confirmed CLAH due to a StAR mutation as the definitive diagnosis.
<Step 8> The final diagnosis is congenital lipoid adrenal hyperplasia (CLAH) due to a homozygous mutation in the StAR gene.
